concepts.title: Source, preview, and saved artifact
concepts.objective: Edit one bounded source file, connect the changed characters to parsed preview output, save it, and resume the exact artifact state.

sourceAnchors.claim: Current benchmark introduces the editor and connects an immediate source edit to preview output.

misconceptions: The preview is the source file, so a visible result proves the saved source is correct.
evidenceRequirements: Changed text must appear in the preview and remain after a reload.
Learner must identify source, parsed output, saved draft, and last passing checkpoint separately.

concepts.title: HTML purpose and boundary
concepts.objective: Explain and demonstrate that HTML describes document content and meaning while CSS handles presentation and JavaScript handles dynamic behavior.

sourceAnchors.locator: Web standards 1.2 and Semantic HTML overview
sourceAnchors.claim: MDN separates HTML structure and semantics from CSS presentation and JavaScript behavior.

misconceptions: HTML is a programming language whose element names directly specify visual styling.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must classify changed requirements as content, semantics, presentation, or behavior.
Artifact must remain understandable before author CSS or JavaScript is added.

concepts.title: Element anatomy
concepts.objective: Identify and author element, start tag, tag name, content, and end tag without confusing any part for the complete element.

sourceAnchors.claim: The standard defines tags as markup delimiters and gives normative start-tag and end-tag syntax.
Learn HTML distinguishes opening tag, closing tag, content, and complete element.

misconceptions: An opening tag by itself is the complete HTML element.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must label anatomy in valid and malformed examples.
Learner must author an unfamiliar normal element from a content requirement without copied source.

concepts.title: Tags, elements, and nodes
concepts.objective: Distinguish source tags from DOM elements and text nodes, then explain why source serialization and parsed structure can differ.

sourceAnchors.claim: HTML documents are node trees containing elements and text nodes rather than a flat sequence of visible tags.

misconceptions: Every tag becomes a separate visible DOM node, including closing tags.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must match source ranges to resulting element and text nodes.
Changed-case inspection must explain one source-to-DOM difference without relying on appearance.

concepts.title: Text nodes and whitespace
concepts.objective: Predict how ordinary text, spaces, line breaks, and reserved characters become text nodes and rendered inline content.

sourceAnchors.locator: HTML syntax text and character references
sourceAnchors.claim: Normal elements may contain text and character references subject to syntax and content-model restrictions.

misconceptions: Indentation and repeated source spaces always render exactly as typed.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must predict DOM text and visible spacing for changed indentation and text.
Artifact must preserve intended words while using elements rather than spaces for document structure.

concepts.title: Nesting and tree relationships
concepts.objective: Author and inspect parent, child, descendant, ancestor, and sibling relationships using valid nesting rather than indentation alone.

sourceAnchors.claim: MDN requires correct nesting because browser repair may produce a different structure than the author intended.

misconceptions: Indentation creates parent-child relationships even when tags close in another order.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must draw and verify the tree produced by nested source.
Malformed close-order case must be repaired from DOM evidence and then survive a changed sibling insertion.

concepts.title: Allowed content relationships
concepts.objective: Use element content models and contextual author requirements to decide whether a parent-child relationship is conforming and meaningful.

sourceAnchors.claim: Elements impose content categories, models, and contextual author restrictions beyond basic tag syntax.

misconceptions: Any element can contain any other element when the browser displays it.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must reject a visually plausible but invalid content relationship and cite the controlling model.
Independent build must pass parsed-relationship checks rather than source token checks.

concepts.title: Void elements
concepts.objective: Recognize and author void elements without invented end tags or XHTML self-closing assumptions, then explain how their attributes provide behavior.

sourceAnchors.claim: Void elements have no end tag; a trailing slash does not make an HTML void element self-closing.

misconceptions: A trailing slash closes any HTML element and prevents later text from becoming its content.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must distinguish normal, void, and foreign-element syntax in changed examples.
Malformed void-element source must be diagnosed from parsed attributes and following content.

concepts.title: Attribute names and values
concepts.objective: Place attributes inside a start tag and author separated names and values using unambiguous quoting and specification-defined names.

sourceAnchors.claim: The standard defines attribute placement, names, values, separators, and four serialization forms.

misconceptions: Attributes belong after an element or inside its closing tag because they describe the complete element.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must parse name, separator, quote, value, and start-tag boundaries in malformed source.
Changed-case build must apply a valid element-specific attribute and verify its parsed value and behavior.

concepts.title: Boolean, enumerated, and text attributes
concepts.objective: Distinguish boolean presence, enumerated keywords, token lists, IDs, URLs, and free text so values follow each attribute contract.

sourceAnchors.locator: Common microsyntaxes and element attribute definitions
sourceAnchors.claim: Attribute meaning depends on its specification-defined value type rather than one universal string rule.

misconceptions: Writing a boolean attribute as false disables it because the word false becomes a false value.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must predict behavior for present, absent, invalid, and changed attribute values.
Artifact must use boolean and enumerated attributes without relying on string truthiness assumptions.

concepts.title: Comments and character references
concepts.objective: Use comments for bounded author notes and character references where literal syntax would be ambiguous, without using either as page structure.

sourceAnchors.locator: HTML syntax comments and character references
sourceAnchors.claim: HTML defines separate comment syntax and character-reference parsing rules.

misconceptions: Commented secrets are safe because users cannot see comments in the rendered page.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must render reserved characters without accidentally creating markup.
Inspection must locate a comment in source and DOM and explain why sensitive data never belongs there.

concepts.title: Browser parsing and recovery
concepts.objective: Compare authored source with the DOM produced by browser error recovery, then repair the cause and verify a conforming stable tree.

sourceAnchors.claim: Browsers construct a DOM through defined parsing and error-recovery behavior rather than displaying source literally.

misconceptions: If malformed HTML looks acceptable in one browser, the source is valid and interoperable.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must predict and inspect recovery for a missing or misplaced tag.
Repair must pass source conformance, expected DOM structure, and a changed insertion case.

concepts.title: Doctype and rendering mode
concepts.objective: Place the HTML doctype first and explain its current rendering-mode purpose without treating it as an HTML element or version declaration.

sourceAnchors.claim: A doctype is required legacy preamble that avoids incompatible rendering modes.
MDN requires understanding both doctype use and its historical status.

misconceptions: The doctype is the root HTML element and tells the browser which modern HTML version to download.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must distinguish doctype node, document element, and rendered content.
Changed document without a correct doctype must expose mode evidence and be repaired.

concepts.title: Complete document structure
concepts.objective: Author a complete document with doctype, html root, metadata head, titled document, and content body from an empty file.

sourceAnchors.claim: A conforming serialized document has a doctype and html document element containing head and body structure.

misconceptions: A body fragment that renders is equivalent to a complete portable HTML document.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must build the complete envelope without receiving hidden scaffold source.
Source, DOM, title-bar, and validation evidence must distinguish head metadata from body content.

concepts.title: Document language
concepts.objective: Declare the primary human language and identify bounded language changes so pronunciation and language processing have programmatic evidence.

sourceAnchors.claim: MDN requires setting document language on the opening html element.
Page language and language changes need programmatic determination.

misconceptions: The lang attribute translates visible text into the chosen language.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must select valid language tags for page and changed inline-language content.
DOM and accessibility evidence must expose primary and changed language scopes.

concepts.title: Character encoding declaration
concepts.objective: Declare UTF-8 early in the head and diagnose text corruption as a bytes-to-characters interpretation problem.

sourceAnchors.claim: HTML constrains encoding declarations and their serialization position.

misconceptions: UTF-8 is a font that visually supports more symbols.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must explain bytes, encoding declaration, characters, and font as separate layers.
Changed non-ASCII content must survive save, reload, parse, and copy evidence.

concepts.title: Document title and bounded metadata
concepts.objective: Write a unique useful document title and only metadata whose consumer and purpose can be named and verified.

sourceAnchors.claim: MDN includes title, search metadata, icons, stylesheets, and script links in head knowledge.

misconceptions: The title element creates the largest visible heading in the page body.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must verify document title separately from visible heading text.
Changed multi-page case must produce distinct meaningful titles without keyword stuffing.

concepts.title: Search and sharing discovery metadata
concepts.objective: Provide accurate descriptions, canonical identity, icons, and bounded sharing metadata whose consumers, limitations, and visible-content relationship can be verified.

sourceAnchors.claim: Document metadata describes the page to browsers, search systems, sharing tools, and other consumers without replacing visible content.

misconceptions: Metadata keywords can guarantee ranking and may describe content that the visible page does not provide.
evidenceRequirements: Changed multi-page cases must expose unique truthful title, description, canonical, icon, and sharing evidence without duplicated identity.
Learner must name each metadata consumer, verify inspectable output where possible, and state what the metadata cannot guarantee.

concepts.title: Viewport configuration
concepts.objective: Configure the layout viewport for responsive design without restricting zoom or treating metadata as a substitute for resilient CSS.

sourceAnchors.claim: Text resizing and reflow must remain available across viewport configuration.

misconceptions: Setting width=device-width automatically makes fixed-width content responsive.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must compare layout viewport evidence before and after safe metadata.
Changed fixed-width page must still fail until CSS constraints are repaired, proving metadata is not the layout solution.

concepts.title: Files, paths, and URL references
concepts.objective: Organize a portable project and resolve relative, root-relative, absolute, and fragment references from the correct base URL.

sourceAnchors.claim: MDN requires absolute and relative paths plus detailed slash, dot, and double-dot syntax.

misconceptions: A relative path is relative to the project folder regardless of the current document location.
evidenceRequirements: Moved-folder and nested-document cases must resolve expected resources without machine-specific paths.
Learner must diagnose case, extension, base, path-segment, and URL-encoding failures from network evidence.

concepts.title: Browser request, parse, and render evidence
concepts.objective: Trace navigation through URL resolution, requests, responses, resource discovery, HTML parsing, DOM construction, CSS matching, layout, paint, and interactive output at a beginner-appropriate level.

sourceAnchors.locator: Requests, responses, HTML handling, CSS handling, and rendering
sourceAnchors.claim: Browsers request multiple resources, parse HTML into a DOM, parse and match CSS, then assemble rendered output.

misconceptions: Opening one HTML file makes the browser read every project file automatically without references or requests.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must connect changed source, requested resources, response failures, DOM nodes, computed styles, and rendered output without collapsing them into one layer.
A missing-resource case must be diagnosed at URL, request, response, parse, match, or render stage using the evidence surface that can prove it.

concepts.title: Technical authority and claim verification
concepts.objective: Turn an HTML or browser question into a bounded claim, locate current specification or official documentation evidence, record version and context, test behavior where appropriate, and preserve uncertainty.

sourceAnchors.locator: Research technical information and web standards outcomes
sourceAnchors.claim: Professional web work requires finding authoritative technical information and separating standards from examples, compatibility observations, and unsupported claims.

misconceptions: The first search result or generated answer is authoritative when its code appears to work in one browser.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must produce a claim-evidence-limit-test record for a changed HTML question using a current authority rather than a copied answer.
Conflicting or outdated evidence must be resolved by version, normative status, browser observation, and an explicit remaining-uncertainty statement.

concepts.title: Links and destinations
concepts.objective: Create anchors whose href values resolve to intended web, file, email, telephone, and same-document destinations with safe behavior.

sourceAnchors.claim: Links are fundamental web navigation and require href, path, state, and link-text understanding.

misconceptions: An anchor without href remains an equivalent keyboard-operable hyperlink.
evidenceRequirements: All changed destinations must resolve and expose correct scheme, path, query, and fragment evidence.
Keyboard and accessibility inspection must identify each functional link and its destination purpose.

concepts.title: Link purpose and fragment targets
concepts.objective: Write destination-specific link text and stable unique fragment targets that remain understandable outside surrounding prose.

sourceAnchors.claim: Link purpose needs context and identifiers must not create ambiguous relationships.

misconceptions: Repeated click-here links are accessible because each appears beside a descriptive paragraph.
evidenceRequirements: Link-list inspection must distinguish destinations without nearby prose.
Changed long-page case must navigate to unique named targets without obscuring focus or heading context.

concepts.title: Image purpose and text alternatives
concepts.objective: Classify informative, functional, decorative, complex, and text images, then provide the appropriate text alternative or exclusion.

sourceAnchors.claim: Alternative treatment depends on image purpose and surrounding content rather than appearance alone.

misconceptions: Every image needs a detailed literal description in its alt attribute.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must justify alternative treatment for changed image purposes.
Missing-image and accessibility-tree checks must preserve equivalent task information without redundant announcements.

concepts.title: Image dimensions and loading behavior
concepts.objective: Provide intrinsic dimensions, appropriate loading behavior, safe sources, and performance evidence without distorting content.

sourceAnchors.claim: MDN includes image dimensions to reduce disruptive layout movement and media optimization.

misconceptions: Width and height attributes force an image to keep that exact visual size at every viewport.
evidenceRequirements: Changed aspect-ratio and missing-resource cases must preserve a stable non-distorted layout.
Learner must separate intrinsic dimensions, rendered CSS dimensions, file bytes, and loading priority evidence.

concepts.title: Figures and captions
concepts.objective: Use figure and figcaption only when self-contained content and its caption form a meaningful relationship.

sourceAnchors.claim: HTML defines contextual figure content and caption relationships.

misconceptions: Every image belongs in a figure and its figcaption replaces alternative text.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must decide when a figure relationship is warranted and when ordinary flow is clearer.
Accessibility inspection must distinguish caption, alternative text, and surrounding explanation roles.

concepts.title: SVG embedding and accessible meaning
concepts.objective: Choose inline SVG, external image, or decorative treatment and expose or hide graphic meaning consistently.

sourceAnchors.claim: Graphics need purpose-dependent text alternatives and must not duplicate nearby content.

misconceptions: SVG path data automatically gives assistive technology a useful accessible name.
evidenceRequirements: Meaningful and decorative changed cases must produce distinct accessibility-tree results.
Learner must justify embedding choice using styling, caching, interaction, security, and accessibility constraints.

concepts.title: Native audio and video
concepts.objective: Provide native controls, suitable sources, dimensions where relevant, fallback content, and user-controlled playback.

sourceAnchors.claim: MDN includes audio, video, sources, controls, muted behavior, optimization, and licensing.

misconceptions: A media file that autoplays successfully is accessible without native controls.
evidenceRequirements: Keyboard and changed-source tests must preserve playback control and fallback information.
Learner must justify autoplay, preload, controls, source, poster, and file-size decisions.

concepts.title: Captions, transcripts, and alternatives
concepts.objective: Provide synchronized captions, transcripts, audio description planning, and equivalent fallback according to media content and task.

sourceAnchors.claim: Prerecorded and live media alternatives depend on audio, video, timing, and conformance context.

misconceptions: Visible subtitles embedded in video always replace a selectable caption track and transcript.
evidenceRequirements: Changed silent-video, audio-only, and dialogue-plus-visual-detail cases must receive appropriate alternatives.
Learner must verify track availability, labels, language, timing, and transcript access.

concepts.title: Embedded document boundaries
concepts.objective: Embed a document with an accurate title, minimal permissions, loading fallback, and responsive bounds while preserving origin awareness.

sourceAnchors.claim: Embedded browsing contexts have distinct documents, origins, names, permissions, and sandbox capabilities.

misconceptions: An iframe title changes the title displayed inside the embedded page and grants no accessibility benefit beyond that.
evidenceRequirements: Accessibility and security inspection must expose embed purpose, origin, sandbox, permissions, and navigation behavior.
Changed untrusted embed case must be refused or constrained rather than granted convenience permissions.

concepts.title: Headings and document hierarchy
concepts.objective: Choose heading ranks from content hierarchy and navigation needs rather than visual size, then verify a coherent outline.

sourceAnchors.claim: MDN requires logical heading levels and rejects choosing rank for appearance.

misconceptions: Every section must contain an h1, or only one h1 may ever appear in a conforming document.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must defend rank choices from a changed content outline with CSS removed.
Heading navigation and accessibility-tree evidence must preserve page and section relationships.

concepts.title: Paragraphs, line breaks, and thematic breaks
concepts.objective: Use paragraphs, line breaks, and thematic breaks according to prose structure rather than visual spacing.

sourceAnchors.claim: HTML assigns different semantics and content rules to paragraphs, line breaks, and thematic breaks.

misconceptions: Multiple br elements are the semantic way to create vertical spacing between unrelated blocks.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must classify changed prose, address-line, poem-line, and topic-shift requirements.
Artifact must preserve meaning with author styles disabled and at changed text length.

concepts.title: Ordered, unordered, and description lists
concepts.objective: Select and correctly nest ordered, unordered, and description lists according to sequence and term-description relationships.

sourceAnchors.claim: MDN distinguishes unordered, ordered, and description lists by relationship and includes navigation use.

misconceptions: Lists differ only in their default bullet or number styling.
evidenceRequirements: Changed sequence, collection, glossary, navigation, and nested-list cases must use appropriate structures.
DOM and accessibility evidence must preserve list type, item count, nesting, terms, and descriptions.

concepts.title: Emphasis and importance
concepts.objective: Use emphasis, strong importance, and presentationally neutral text according to linguistic meaning rather than desired font style.

sourceAnchors.claim: MDN requires correct use of emphasis, importance, and other text-level semantics.

misconceptions: Strong and em are interchangeable shortcuts for bold and italic appearance.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must select semantics for changed stress, warning, label, and purely visual requirements.
Artifact must retain intended meaning when author styles and visual emphasis are removed.

concepts.title: Quotations and citations
concepts.objective: Represent block and inline quotations, sources, and creative-work citations without inventing unsupported provenance.

sourceAnchors.claim: MDN includes quotations and citations among required text semantics.

misconceptions: The cite element is the correct container for a quoted speaker or any source URL.
evidenceRequirements: Changed inline quote, long quotation, title, speaker, and source cases must separate content roles.
Learner must verify visible attribution and machine-readable relationships without fabricating metadata.

concepts.title: Dates, abbreviations, code, and contact context
concepts.objective: Use time, abbreviation, code-related, address, superscript, subscript, deletion, and insertion semantics where their defined meaning fits.

sourceAnchors.claim: MDN expects awareness and appropriate use of dates, abbreviations, addresses, code, and related text semantics.

misconceptions: The address element can mark up any street or postal address wherever it appears.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must select or reject specialized text semantics in changed editorial contexts.
Machine-readable values must remain valid while visible localized text changes.

concepts.title: Page landmarks
concepts.objective: Use header, nav, main, aside, and footer according to page relationships and verify useful non-duplicative landmark navigation.

sourceAnchors.claim: Landmarks identify major page regions for navigation when element context and accessible names are appropriate.

misconceptions: Every header and footer element always creates a banner or contentinfo landmark.
evidenceRequirements: Accessibility-tree inspection must expose one useful main region and distinguish nested contextual headers and footers.
Changed repeated navigation regions must receive unique purpose or names without redundant landmarks.

concepts.title: Sections, articles, and generic groups
concepts.objective: Choose section, article, div, and other groups from content relationships, heading needs, reuse, and semantics rather than styling convenience.

sourceAnchors.claim: MDN contrasts generic div containers with semantic structural elements and their accessibility benefits.

misconceptions: Section is a semantic replacement for every div and automatically fixes document structure.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must justify grouping choices for changed standalone, thematic, navigation, and styling-only content.
Heading and landmark evidence must match intended relationships without unnecessary regions.

concepts.title: Native disclosure
concepts.objective: Use details and summary when native disclosure behavior meets the need, and reject them when menu, dialog, or exclusive selection behavior is required.

sourceAnchors.claim: HTML defines disclosure content, summary labeling, and native open state behavior.

misconceptions: Details and summary are universal replacements for dropdown menus, accordions, and dialogs.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must choose or reject native disclosure for changed interaction requirements.
Keyboard, focus, open-state, label, and no-script behavior must pass.

concepts.title: Native interaction before custom roles
concepts.objective: Select native links, buttons, disclosures, and form controls before custom behavior unless a documented user need proves native behavior insufficient.

sourceAnchors.claim: MDN emphasizes native buttons and form elements because browsers provide keyboard, focus, and semantic behavior.

misconceptions: A div with role button is equivalent to button after adding one click handler.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must inventory semantics, keyboard, focus, states, submission, and disabled behavior before choosing custom interaction.
Changed pointer-free and script-failure cases must retain the intended task where native behavior can provide it.

concepts.title: Form purpose and submitted data
concepts.objective: Build a form whose action, method, control names, values, and submit behavior match a bounded data-collection purpose.

sourceAnchors.claim: MDN includes form submission, GET and POST, control names, values, and native behavior.

misconceptions: Every visible enabled form value is submitted even when its control has no name.
evidenceRequirements: Changed submission must produce the expected name-value set and omit disabled or unnamed controls by rule.
Learner must justify method and data minimization without claiming client HTML secures server processing.

concepts.title: Labels, instructions, and descriptions
concepts.objective: Provide persistent labels and programmatic instructions or descriptions so every control purpose and format remains available across states.

sourceAnchors.claim: Controls need explicit labels and instructions associated in programmatically determinable ways.

misconceptions: Placeholder text is an equivalent accessible label because it appears inside the control.
evidenceRequirements: Accessibility-name and description inspection must identify every changed control without visual proximity.
Labels and shared instructions must remain persistent under zoom, autofill, entered values, and validation errors.

concepts.title: Input purpose, keyboard, and autocomplete
concepts.objective: Choose input type, autocomplete token, inputmode, name, and value constraints from data meaning without blocking realistic international input.

sourceAnchors.claim: MDN requires common input types, attributes, validation, and form states.

misconceptions: Input type only changes appearance and never affects semantics, keyboard, validation, or submission.
evidenceRequirements: Changed data examples must accept valid edge cases and reject impossible values for the right reason.
Learner must verify accessible purpose, browser keyboard hint, autocomplete behavior, and submitted value separately.

concepts.title: Radio, checkbox, and grouped choices
concepts.objective: Use fieldset, legend, radio names, checkbox independence, labels, and values to expose exclusive and multiple-choice relationships.

sourceAnchors.claim: Related controls need group structure and labels that communicate shared purpose and choice relationships.

misconceptions: Visually adjacent radio buttons automatically form an announced exclusive group.
evidenceRequirements: Keyboard and accessibility checks must expose group name, option names, state, and exclusive or independent behavior.
Changed default, missing-value, and dynamically reordered choices must submit the intended data.

concepts.title: Text areas, selections, and button types
concepts.objective: Use textarea, select, option groups, and explicit button types with correct labels, initial values, and submission behavior.

sourceAnchors.claim: MDN includes textarea, select, option, button types, and warns against routine reset buttons.

misconceptions: A button inside a form does nothing unless a click handler explicitly assigns behavior.
evidenceRequirements: Changed form must distinguish submit, reset risk, and ordinary button behavior without accidental submission.
Selected, disabled, multiple, placeholder-like, and submitted option states must be verified.

concepts.title: Constraint validation
concepts.objective: Apply required, length, range, and pattern constraints only where they match realistic data, then inspect validity and browser behavior.

sourceAnchors.claim: MDN treats native constraint validation as usability support, not a substitute for server validation.

misconceptions: The strictest regular expression always improves data quality and secures submitted data.
evidenceRequirements: Valid diverse and invalid changed cases must produce correct validity states without excluding legitimate users.
Learner must explain client usability, server authority, disabled behavior, and bypass boundaries.

concepts.title: Form error identification and recovery
concepts.objective: Provide specific persistent errors, programmatic relationships, summary or focus strategy, retained valid data, and successful correction.

sourceAnchors.claim: Input errors need identification, instructions, suggestions, prevention, and accessible authentication or redundant-entry treatment by context.

misconceptions: A red border and failed submission provide sufficient error identification for all users.
evidenceRequirements: Keyboard, screen-reader, zoom, and changed multi-error cases must identify, locate, explain, and correct every error.
Successful retry must retain unrelated valid values and produce submitted evidence without duplicate side effects.

concepts.title: Data tables versus layout
concepts.objective: Choose a table only for two-dimensional data relationships and reject table markup for page layout or unrelated item lists.

sourceAnchors.claim: WAI limits table guidance to grid data and rejects tables as a layout technique.

misconceptions: Any aligned columns should use table markup because tables guarantee responsive layout.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must classify changed grid data, layout, comparison cards, and key-value content.
Chosen structure must remain understandable under linearized and alternative rendering.

concepts.title: Rows, cells, groups, and captions
concepts.objective: Build table, caption, row groups, rows, header cells, and data cells that preserve intended data dimensions.

sourceAnchors.claim: MDN includes basic table cells, spanning, groups, captions, and accessible structure.

misconceptions: Bold text in the first row is equivalent to header-cell semantics.
evidenceRequirements: Parsed table grid must match changed row, column, span, and group requirements.
Caption and group structure must remain meaningful with author styles disabled.

concepts.title: Header associations
concepts.objective: Use th, scope, and explicit headers relationships according to simple and complex table structure so each data cell retains context.

sourceAnchors.locator: Tables one-header, two-header, and multi-level tutorials
sourceAnchors.claim: Header and data cells need programmatic relationships, with explicit associations for complex cases.

misconceptions: Scope belongs on data cells because it points each value toward its header.
evidenceRequirements: Representative cell inspection must report correct row and column headers after changed spans or groups.
Learner must choose simple scope or explicit id and headers without overcomplicating a simple table.

concepts.title: Native semantics and accessibility information
concepts.objective: Trace HTML element, attribute, content, and relationship choices into role, accessible name, description, state, property, and structure evidence.

sourceAnchors.claim: Semantic HTML provides browser accessibility hooks including names, tables, alternatives, keyboard use, and source order.

misconceptions: Visible text and visual grouping automatically produce the same accessibility information.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must predict and inspect role, name, description, state, and relationships for changed native markup.
Artifact must remain operable and understandable when visual styling is removed or altered.

concepts.title: Source order and keyboard path
concepts.objective: Keep reading, focus, and interaction order logical from source structure without positive tabindex or visual reordering dependence.

sourceAnchors.claim: Meaningful sequence, keyboard operation, focus order, and unobscured focus need observable evidence.

misconceptions: CSS visual order or positive tabindex safely fixes any source-order problem.
evidenceRequirements: Keyboard-only changed flow must follow meaningful order, expose visible focus, and avoid traps.
Linearized source and accessibility order must preserve task meaning across responsive layouts.

concepts.title: ARIA role, state, and property boundary
concepts.objective: Use ARIA only when native semantics cannot express a required relationship or state, and supply the complete interaction contract.

sourceAnchors.claim: Custom widget roles require coordinated names, states, properties, focus, and keyboard interaction.

misconceptions: Adding an ARIA role automatically gives a generic element native keyboard behavior and state management.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must reject unnecessary ARIA and identify missing behavior in partial custom-widget cases.
Any accepted custom pattern must pass complete role, name, state, property, focus, keyboard, and changed-state checks.

concepts.title: Validation, source, DOM, and accessibility inspection
concepts.objective: Use conformance validation, source, DOM, accessibility, network, and console evidence to locate root cause rather than guess from appearance.

sourceAnchors.claim: MDN requires validation, view source, and DOM inspection for markup debugging.

misconceptions: A clean validator result proves correct semantics, accessibility, links, content, and task behavior.
evidenceRequirements: Learner must select the evidence surface that can falsify each changed defect hypothesis.
Repair record must distinguish source conformance, parsed behavior, accessibility information, and user-task verification.

concepts.title: Changed-content and failure testing
concepts.objective: Test HTML with long and translated text, missing resources, duplicate identifiers, invalid input, keyboard use, zoom, and altered content relationships.

sourceAnchors.locator: Conformance requirements and applicable success criteria
sourceAnchors.claim: Accessibility requirements apply to complete processes and responsive variations rather than one ideal screenshot.

misconceptions: Passing the supplied example proves the markup will survive realistic content and user variation.
evidenceRequirements: Hidden changed cases must alter content, order, resources, identifiers, input, and viewport without changing the objective.
Learner must explain which invariant each test protects and diagnose at least one rejected near-miss.

concepts.title: Independent HTML transfer and defense
concepts.objective: Design, build, test, and defend an unfamiliar multi-section HTML information and data-collection artifact from an empty project.

sourceAnchors.locator: HTML certification project and review blocks
sourceAnchors.claim: Current benchmark requires independent HTML project, review, quiz, and cumulative examination evidence.
Core HTML competence spans documents, structure, text, links, media, controls, forms, tables, and debugging.

misconceptions: Completing a project from a nearly identical workshop starter proves independent HTML transfer.
evidenceRequirements: Artifact must begin from empty project constraints and pass behavior, accessibility, failure, changed-content, and validation evidence.
Learner must defend element choices, relationships, alternatives, form data, table context, test limits, and remaining uncertainty.

architectureFindings: Current blueprint delays first HTML construction behind thirteen tooling activities; files, browser, DevTools, and research must enter just in time around the first artifact.

Current basic HTML and semantic HTML boundaries separate concepts that depend on one another; proposed modules follow element, document, content, interaction, data, access, and transfer prerequisites.

Current generated activities map all competencies across broad source blocks, but this does not prove each concept receives explicit explanation, malformed cases, fading, debugging, retrieval, or independent transfer.
Current HTML project follows existing generated work and cannot count as transfer until its scenario, empty starter, requirements, grading, and defense differ meaningfully from instruction.
gaps: Subject-matter review must verify each locator, standards claim, prerequisite edge, misconception, and scope boundary.
Instructional and assessment review must map each concept into original activities and inspect full evidence progression.
Accessibility review and representative beginner observation must test the sequence, editor, feedback, recovery, and independent project.
